name: Tlazolteotl
description: Tlazolteotl, also known as Tlaelquani ('Filth Eater'), is a complex and multifaceted goddess within the Aztec pantheon, primarily associated with purification, confession, and midwifery. She embodies both the allure and the consequences of transgression, particularly sexual misconduct. Her role as a 'filth eater' signifies her capacity to absolve individuals of their sins through ritual confession, thereby cleansing them and restoring balance to their lives. Tlazolteotl is often depicted with distinctive facial markings and wearing attire that emphasizes her association with fertility and childbirth. Furthermore, she is linked to the lunar cycle and the cyclical nature of life, death, and rebirth. While feared for her connection to sin, she is also revered for her ability to grant forgiveness and facilitate new beginnings, making her a crucial figure in the Aztec understanding of morality and spiritual well-being. Her worship involved elaborate rituals and offerings aimed at securing her favor and ensuring a prosperous life.
subtitle: Goddess of Filth and Purification

keyMyths.title: The Origin of Tlazolteotl's Power
keyMyths.description: According to some accounts, Tlazolteotl gained her power over sin and purification by consuming the filth and transgressions of humanity. This act, while repulsive, allowed her to understand the nature of sin and develop the means to cleanse it. The myth highlights the burden she carries and the sacrifice she makes to maintain cosmic balance, emphasizing the complex duality of her nature as both a source of sin and its ultimate purifier. This myth reinforces her role as a pivotal figure in Aztec moral and spiritual life.
keyMyths.source: Sahagún, Florentino. *General History of the Things of New Spain*.

keyMyths.title: The Rite of Confession
keyMyths.description: Aztec priests conducted confessions before Tlazolteotl, offering individuals a chance to unburden themselves of their sins. This ritual was viewed as a crucial step towards spiritual healing and social reintegration. The process involved recounting transgressions to a priest acting as Tlazolteotl's representative, followed by prescribed penances or offerings. The myth underscores the importance of accountability and the possibility of redemption within the Aztec worldview.
keyMyths.source: Miller, Mary, and Karl Taube. *The Gods and Symbols of Ancient Mexico and the Maya*.

keyMyths.title: Tlazolteotl and Childbirth
keyMyths.description: Tlazolteotl's association with childbirth stems from her role in cleansing the mother after the act of giving birth. The process of childbirth was considered a dangerous and transformative event, fraught with spiritual impurities. Tlazolteotl's presence ensured the safe delivery of the child and the purification of the mother, highlighting her connection to both the physical and spiritual aspects of life and death. This myth demonstrates the goddess's protective role and her importance in the continuation of the Aztec lineage.
keyMyths.source: Nicholson, Henry B. *A Guide to Aztec Religion*.
